response: The retired West Indies cricketer aims to optimize her local team's performance using data-driven strategies. To find the optimal number of practice sessions, we analyze the quadratic equation for the average batting score, \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). This equation represents a downward-opening parabola, meaning the maximum value occurs at the vertex. The vertex of a parabola defined by \( ax^2 + bx + c \) is located at \( n = -b / (2a) \). In this specific model, \( a \) equals -0.2 and \( b \) equals 5. Calculating the value gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \), which simplifies to \( n = -5 / -0.4 \). The result is exactly 12.5 practice sessions per month. Since partial sessions are difficult to schedule, we check integers 12 and 13 to find the true maximum. Plugging \( n = 12 \) into the equation yields \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). Plugging \( n = 13 \) yields \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both values produce the same peak score, so the maximum average batting score is 81.2. For Sub-problem 2, we calculate the win probability change when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, find \( S \) at \( n = 5 \): \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 5 = 70 \). Next, find \( S \) at \( n = 10 \): \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 20 = 80 \). Now apply the logistic formula \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \). For \( S_5 = 70 \), the exponent is \( -(7 - 3) = -4 \), so \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 0.9820 \).
